quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 14 Quiz 9
quiz.subtitle: 14.9: Cryptography with Matrices
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Encoding and decoding using matrix multiplication

questions.question: Using A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26, encode 'HI' as a column matrix and encrypt with $E = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the ciphertext $C = EM$.

Ciphertext $C = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 13 \end{bmatrix}$ was encoded with $E = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Use $E^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ to decode and find the message (A=1, B=2, ...).
Use $E = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $E^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. Encode 'AB' (A=1, B=2), then decode the ciphertext to verify.
A student tries to use $E = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$ as an encoding matrix. Find the determinant. Can it be used?
questions.explanation: $\det = 2(2) - 4(1) = 4 - 4 = 0$. Singular — no inverse exists, so messages could not be decoded.

questions.question: If a message is 'CAB' and the mapping is A=1, B=2, C=3, what is the message matrix $M$ as a $3 \times 1$ column?
questions.explanation: C=3, A=1, B=2. Placed in order into a column matrix.

questions.question: Decode $C = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 8 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$ using $E = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $E^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Convert to letters (A=1, B=2, ...).
Use $E = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $E^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$. Encode 'HI' (H=8, I=9), then decode to verify.
What is the primary advantage of using a $3 \times 3$ encoding matrix over a $2 \times 2$ matrix?
options: It provides higher security by encoding letters in larger blocks.
It is faster to calculate manually.
It does not require an inverse for decoding.
It can only be used for the first three letters of a word.
questions.explanation: Larger matrices increase the complexity of relationships between encoded characters, making the code harder to break without the key.

scoreMessages.90: Master Cryptographer! You understand matrix-based security — encoding messages, decoding with the inverse, verifying the full cycle, identifying invalid encoding matrices, and working with both 2×2 and 3×3 systems.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Key reminders: (1) Encoding: $C = EM$, (2) Decoding: $M = E^{-1}C$, (3) The encoding matrix must be square with non-zero determinant to be invertible, (4) Map letters to numbers first, then form the message matrix.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Multiplying the encoding matrix by the message matrix, (2) Finding $E^{-1}$ for 2×2 matrices, (3) Padding messages with zeros or spaces to fill the matrix, (4) Converting numbers back to letters after decoding.
scoreMessages.0: Review matrix cryptography: (1) Assign numbers to letters (A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26), (2) Form the message matrix $M$ as a column, (3) Encode: $C = EM$ using an invertible encoding matrix, (4) Decode: $M = E^{-1}C$, (5) $E$ must have non-zero determinant so $E^{-1}$ exists.
